Party_Details.Name: ABB Holdings Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: The three plaintiffs are part of a worldwide group of companies which bear the name ABB and which manufacture, market and sell several products for industrial and commercial uses.
The products of the ABB Group include low, medium and high voltage circuit breakers.
The plaintiffs are part of the Singapore component of the ABB Group, referred to collectively as the ABB Singapore Group.
The first plaintiff is owned by ABB Asea Brown Boveri Ltd, a Swiss company, and was set up in Singapore to be the holding company of the Singapore businesses.
The first plaintiff is the sole shareholder of the second and third plaintiffs.
The first plaintiff is a holding company and does not itself undertake commercial activity.
The plaintiffs commenced this action in December 2007.

The plaintiffs asserted that by reason of his employment with the second and third plaintiffs, the defendant owed all the plaintiffs certain express and implied fiduciary and other duties and that he had breached those duties thus causing damage to the plaintiffs.
By consent of the parties, the trial was focused solely on the issues relating to the defendant’s liability, with quantum to be determined in a separate hearing if liability was established.

The plaintiffs pleaded in paragraph 9 of the statement of claim that various express terms in the employment contracts governed the defendant’s conduct, including duties to promote the plaintiffs’ interests, avoid conflicts, refrain from competing business activities, and maintain secrecy and confidentiality.

In paragraph 10 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs alleged implied duties owed by the defendant, including duties of good faith and fidelity, duties not to misuse confidential information or trade secrets, duties to disclose potential threats or competitive risks, and duties not to make improper use of information for personal advantage or to cause detriment to the company.

In paragraph 11 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs pleaded in the alternative that the defendant owed them fiduciary duties, including duties to act in good faith and in the plaintiffs’ best interests, to avoid conflicts, and to disclose information and potential threats relevant to the plaintiffs.
The plaintiffs did not specify in their statement of claim how each pleaded particular of breach correlated with each alleged duty.

The plaintiffs alleged that the defendant breached his duties by his involvement with Great Vision International Pte Ltd, Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd, Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co. Ltd, Gelpag International Pte Ltd, Gelpag mbH, and by failing to disclose communications with XIHARI and a RMB70,000 loan from Xiamen Fu Cheng Real Estate Property Development Co. Ltd.

The plaintiffs alleged that within a few months after its incorporation in December 2002, Huadian made rapid strides in the manufacture, marketing, approval and sale of switchgears and circuit breakers similar to those of the plaintiffs and the ABB Group.

The plaintiffs relied on an Employee Handbook used by the ABB Singapore Group which contained policies, terms and conditions applicable to all employees, including provisions on private participation in other business activities, transfer and deployment, protection of company assets and information, and avoidance of conflicts of interest.

The Employee Handbook stated that every employee had the duty to protect company assets, confidential and proprietary information, and to avoid any conflict between personal interests and those of the company.

The Employee Handbook provided that employees had a duty to avoid financial, business or other relationships which might be opposed to the interests of the company or might cause a conflict with the performance of their duties.

The plaintiffs contended that the reference in the defendant’s first employment contract to the General Terms and Conditions of Employment was a reference to the terms and conditions set out in the Employee Handbook and that these terms applied to all three of the defendant’s employment contracts.

The plaintiffs relied on the evidence of Mr Sim Boon Kiat, a former President and Country Manager of the third plaintiff, that the Employee Handbook was a live document with regular updating and that it applied to all employees of the ABB Singapore Group.

The plaintiffs relied on the defendant’s admission in cross-examination that he was aware of and had access to the Employee Handbook, that it formed the General Terms and Conditions of his employment, and that it should govern his practice in the companies.

The plaintiffs submitted that the defendant owed fiduciary duties to the second and third plaintiffs as a very senior employee and that his senior management role was equivalent in fiduciary responsibility to that of a director.

The plaintiffs alleged that the defendant’s approach to former ABB technical expert Guenther Leonhardt on behalf of XIHARI to assist in developing a new generation of medium voltage switchgear and circuit breakers was against the interests of the plaintiffs and the ABB Group.
The plaintiffs alleged that the defendant failed to disclose to them XIHARI’s plans to develop and market new medium voltage products and the potential competitive threat posed by XIHARI and Huadian.

The plaintiffs alleged that the defendant was involved in the setting up of Huadian while still employed by the third plaintiff and that he arranged for his former colleagues and associates to establish Huadian as a competing business.

The plaintiffs relied on an interview published in October 2003 in which the defendant was quoted as saying that towards the end of 2001 preparations to set up Huadian had begun and that these preparations involved multiple parties including Fu Hsiang, XIHARI and a German company.

The plaintiffs relied on statements on Huadian’s website that Huadian was established in December 2002 by a German company and partners from China and Singapore and that it was aggressive in developing global markets including Southeast Asia.

The plaintiffs relied on company records of Xiamen Fu Cheng showing an outstanding amount of RMB70,000 recorded as a travel expenses loan to the defendant, supported by a payment voucher dated 3 December 2002 naming the defendant as payee and applicant.

The plaintiffs pointed out that Huadian had a registered capital of RMB30 million and that a 16.2% shareholding acquired by Gelpag mbH would have cost RMB4.86 million, of which approximately RMB4.374 million represented the defendant’s indirect investment through his majority shareholding in Gelpag mbH.
The defendant agreed in cross-examination that about S$900,000 of his investment in Gelpag mbH came from profits he had made from the Xiamen property development project.

The plaintiffs alleged that the defendant’s substantial profit from the Xiamen project indicated that he had been directly or indirectly involved in that project while still employed by the third plaintiff.
The plaintiffs alleged that the defendant breached his contractual prohibition on private participation in other business activities by his involvement in Great Vision and Xiamen Fu Cheng.

The plaintiffs alleged that the defendant breached his duties by assisting in the incorporation of Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd and allowing his home address to be used as its registered office while he was still employed by the third plaintiff.

The plaintiffs alleged that Huadian’s medium voltage products were very similar to ABB’s products in terms of design, specifications, layout and function and that Huadian competed strongly with ABB’s products in overlapping markets.

The plaintiffs relied on the affidavit of Markus Heimbach, Group Senior Vice-President and Head of Business Unit Medium Voltage Products of ABB Management Services Ltd, to describe similarities between ABB’s medium voltage products and Huadian’s products.

The plaintiffs relied on a series of nine e-mails exchanged between the defendant and Mr Leonhardt between 3 December 2002 and 3 February 2003 concerning XIHARI’s interest in engaging Mr Leonhardt as a technical adviser and technical controller for a new manufacturing company.
The plaintiffs contended that the defendant knew or ought to have known that XIHARI and Huadian posed a potential threat and competitive risk to the plaintiffs’ business.

The plaintiffs suggested that the defendant’s failure to disclose XIHARI’s activities and the Huadian project was prompted by his own involvement and vested interest in Huadian, which he did not want the plaintiffs to discover.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendant, a former senior manager and director within the ABB Singapore Group, owed contractual, implied and fiduciary duties to the plaintiffs and breached those duties by participating in or assisting competing or unrelated businesses, by failing to disclose potential competitive threats, and by assisting in the establishment and development of Huadian and related entities.
Party_Details.Rule: Contractual duties arise from the express and implied terms of the defendant’s employment contracts, including terms incorporated from the Employee Handbook requiring protection of company assets and confidential information, avoidance of conflicts of interest, and refraining from private participation in other business activities; fiduciary duties analogous to those owed by directors may be owed by very senior employees and include duties to act in good faith in the company’s best interests, to avoid conflicts between duty and personal interest, and to disclose to the company information and activities that may damage its interests.
Party_Details.Application: The court examined the three successive employment contracts and the Employee Handbook to determine which express and implied terms governed the defendant’s employment and whether those terms extended to the first plaintiff as a holding company; it then considered whether the defendant’s senior management roles within the ABB Singapore Group gave rise to fiduciary duties to each plaintiff and whether his conduct in relation to Great Vision, Xiamen Fu Cheng, Webmoney, XIHARI, Huadian, Gelpag International and Gelpag mbH fell within the scope of those duties or the contractual prohibitions on private participation and conflicts of interest.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The court held that the first plaintiff, as a non-operating holding company with no direct employment relationship with the defendant, was not owed fiduciary duties by him and that the first plaintiff’s claim against the defendant should be dismissed, with costs to be fixed after submissions on quantum.

Party_Details.Name: ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: The second plaintiff, ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd, manufactures miniature circuit breakers, which are low voltage products.
The second plaintiff is a wholly owned subsidiary of the first plaintiff within the ABB Singapore Group.
The second plaintiff’s business relates to low voltage products and its market area includes Singapore and South East Asia.

By January 2001, the second plaintiff had transferred all its business activities to other entities except that it remained the holding company of facilities in Batam and Jakarta which assembled miniature circuit breakers.
The manufacturing facility in Batam owned by the second plaintiff closed down in 2002.
The defendant was President of the second plaintiff from January 1997 to January 2000.
The defendant was a director of the second plaintiff from July 1998 to February 2003.
As a director of the second plaintiff, the defendant occupied a fiduciary position vis-à-vis the second plaintiff and owed it fiduciary duties.

The defendant’s first employment contract dated 19 July 1996 was made between the first plaintiff and the defendant and covered the period from 1 January 1997 to 31 January 2000, during which his position was President of the second plaintiff.

Clause 1 of the first employment contract provided that the defendant’s appointment was subject to the General Terms and Conditions of Employment of the Country Organisation and that he would faithfully serve as President of the second plaintiff and use his best endeavours to promote the interest and welfare of the ABB Group.
The second employment contract dated 20 September 1999, effective from 1 February 2000, stated that the defendant’s employing entity was the second plaintiff and that his position was President.

The second employment contract required the defendant to maintain and preserve secrecy in all office matters, not to divulge information to outsiders, and stated that private participation in other business activities, especially in the company’s line of business, was strictly not allowed.
The second employment contract reserved to management the right to review, change, amend, delete or add to the policies, terms and conditions of employment as management deemed fit.

The third employment contract, effective retrospectively from 1 January 2001, transferred the defendant’s employment from the second plaintiff to the third plaintiff but did not otherwise change his rights, privileges or terms and conditions of employment.
After the transfer of employment to the third plaintiff, the defendant remained a director of the second plaintiff until February 2003.

The court found that the relevant parts of the Employee Handbook were intended to form part of the defendant’s employment contract throughout his years as an employee of the ABB Singapore Group, including during his tenure with the second plaintiff.

The court found that the defendant owed the second plaintiff fiduciary duties including duties to act in good faith and in the company’s best interests, to avoid placing himself in a position of conflict between duty and personal interest, to disclose to the company information of concern and potential threats or competitive risks, and not to misuse information acquired by virtue of his position.
The plaintiffs alleged that the defendant’s involvement with XIHARI and Huadian and his failure to disclose their activities breached his fiduciary duties to the second plaintiff.

The plaintiffs alleged that the defendant’s assistance to XIHARI in securing the services of former ABB technical expert Guenther Leonhardt to develop new medium voltage products was contrary to the interests of the second plaintiff.
Huadian was incorporated on 4 December 2002 as a joint venture between XIHARI and Fu Hsiang International Pte Ltd, a Singapore-incorporated company.
By March 2003, the defendant had been appointed general manager of Huadian, and by March 2004 he became a director of Huadian through Gelpag mbH’s shareholding.

The plaintiffs alleged that Huadian’s rapid development and marketing of medium voltage circuit breakers and switchgears similar to ABB’s products within months of its incorporation indicated misuse of ABB-related know-how to the detriment of the second plaintiff.
The court accepted evidence that the second plaintiff was not in the medium voltage product business and that its business related only to low voltage products.

The court noted that whether the defendant’s later activities in relation to Huadian breached any fiduciary duties to the second plaintiff was uncertain because the second plaintiff was not in the medium voltage product business.

The court found that the defendant’s breaches relating to XIHARI and Huadian were directed at potential competition in medium voltage products rather than at the second plaintiff’s low voltage business.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendant, as a director and former President of the second plaintiff, owed fiduciary and contractual duties to the second plaintiff and breached those duties by assisting XIHARI and Huadian in developing and marketing medium voltage products and by failing to disclose XIHARI’s plans and potential competitive threats.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors owe fiduciary duties to their companies, including duties to act in good faith in the company’s best interests, to avoid conflicts between duty and personal interest, and to disclose to the company information and activities that may damage its interests; very senior employees may owe similar fiduciary duties; contractual terms from the employment contracts and incorporated Employee Handbook also require avoidance of conflicts and protection of company interests, but the scope of any duty must be commensurate with the company’s actual business.
Party_Details.Application: The court held that the defendant, as a director, owed fiduciary duties to the second plaintiff, but it also found that the second plaintiff’s business was confined to low voltage products and that it did not operate in the medium voltage product market in which XIHARI and Huadian were active; the alleged breaches related primarily to medium voltage products and potential competition in that segment, and there was insufficient nexus between those activities and the second plaintiff’s low voltage business to establish actionable breach of fiduciary duty causing loss to the second plaintiff.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The court found that while the defendant owed fiduciary duties to the second plaintiff as a director, the established breaches related to medium voltage activities did not clearly infringe those duties in the context of the second plaintiff’s low voltage business, but the court nonetheless granted judgment in favour of the second plaintiff for either damages to be assessed or an account of profits to be taken, at the second plaintiff’s election, with the precise impact on the second plaintiff to be addressed at the assessment stage.

Party_Details.Name: Sher Hock Guan Charles

Facts.Text: The defendant, Sher Hock Guan Charles, is a Singaporean who worked for various companies in the ABB Group between 1990 and February 2003.
In the course of his career with the ABB Group, the defendant worked in Xiamen, China for ABB China Ltd and ABB Xiamen Switchgear Co Ltd.
From January 1997 until he left the ABB Group, the defendant was based in Singapore.
Between January 1997 and January 2000, the defendant held the position of President of the second plaintiff.
Between July 1998 and February 2003, the defendant was a director of the second plaintiff.
Between January 2001 and February 2003, the defendant was General Manager of the third plaintiff.
Between September 2001 and February 2003, the defendant was Vice-President and Head of Business Area Low Voltage Products, Automation Technology Products Division Asia South, in the third plaintiff.

The defendant’s first employment contract, dated 19 July 1996, covered the period from 1 January 1997 to 31 January 2000 and was between the first plaintiff and the defendant, with the defendant serving as President of the second plaintiff.

Clause 1 of the first contract provided that the defendant’s appointment was subject to the General Terms and Conditions of Employment of the Country Organisation and that he would use his best endeavours to promote the interest and welfare of the ABB Group.

The second employment contract, dated 20 September 1999 and effective from 1 February 2000, was on the first plaintiff’s letterhead, signed by both the first and second plaintiffs, and stated that the employing entity was the second plaintiff and that the defendant’s position was President.

The second contract required the defendant to maintain and preserve secrecy in all office matters, not to divulge information to outsiders, and stated that private participation in other business activities, especially in the company’s line of business, was strictly not allowed.

The third employment contract, dated 15 January 2001, transferred the defendant’s employment from the second plaintiff to the third plaintiff with retrospective effect from 1 January 2001 and specified his designation and job grade.
The Summary attached to the third contract contained terms relating to leave and benefits and repeated the management’s right to review and amend employment policies and terms.

The defendant initially claimed not to know what the General Terms and Conditions of Employment were but later admitted that he was aware of and had access to the Employee Handbook and that it formed the General Terms and Conditions of his employment.
The defendant agreed in cross-examination that the Employee Handbook should govern his practice in the companies.
The defendant maintained that he regarded the Employee Handbook as containing policies and guidelines rather than contractual terms, although he accepted that it applied to him as an employee.

The defendant denied that the express terms pleaded by the plaintiffs in paragraph 9 of the statement of claim, beyond those expressly set out in his employment contracts, formed part of his employment terms.
The defendant denied owing the implied duties alleged in paragraph 10 of the statement of claim and the fiduciary duties alleged in paragraph 11, save for the express obligations in his contracts.

The defendant contended that the second and third contracts did not contain any obligation to use his best endeavours to promote the interests and welfare of the ABB Group and that such a term should not be implied.
The defendant argued that he did not owe fiduciary duties to the first plaintiff because he was never employed by it and it was merely a holding company with no commercial activity.

The defendant argued that his fiduciary duties as a director of the second plaintiff had to be determined in the context of the second plaintiff’s low voltage business and that it was not involved in medium voltage products.
The defendant contended that his positions in the third plaintiff as General Manager and Vice-President did not make him a fiduciary and that he owed only duties of fidelity as an employee.
After leaving the third plaintiff in February 2003, the defendant joined Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co. Ltd as its General Manager with effect from 3 March 2003.
In March 2004, after Gelpag mbH acquired shares in Huadian, the defendant became Managing Director of Huadian.
Huadian is a manufacturer of switchgears and circuit breakers, particularly medium voltage circuit breakers.
The defendant asserted that between January 1997 and February 2003 he was involved only in the business of low voltage products and had no involvement in medium and high voltage products.
The defendant asserted that he had no access to confidential technical information or confidential information pertaining to the manufacturing process of medium or high voltage products.

The defendant asserted that Great Vision International Pte Ltd and Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd were not engaged in any business in competition with the plaintiffs or which would affect their interests.

The defendant asserted that in incorporating Great Vision and Webmoney he had not intended to and had not competed with the plaintiffs or placed himself in any position of conflict of interest or breach of obligation under his employment contracts.
The defendant asserted that his correspondence with Mr Leonhardt was not in breach of any duty owed to the plaintiffs.

The defendant asserted that the sum of RMB70,000 recorded in Xiamen Fu Cheng’s accounts was a cash advance for travelling expenses of his brother and a former director of Great Vision and that it was wrongly attributed to him.

The defendant asserted that there were no non-competition clauses or restrictive covenants in his employment agreements and that his incorporation of Gelpag International Pte Ltd and his role in Huadian did not breach any duty owed to the plaintiffs.
The defendant asserted that he had no part in the setting up of Huadian and that Huadian’s success did not result from any breach by him of his duties to the plaintiffs.

The defendant passed a property development opportunity in Xiamen, China, to his brother and associates, which led to the incorporation of Great Vision International Pte Ltd in February 2002 and its subsidiary Xiamen Fu Cheng Real Estate Property Development Co. Ltd.
The defendant later became a director and shareholder of Great Vision on 25 April 2003, after leaving the third plaintiff’s employment.

The defendant assisted in the incorporation of Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd on 24 May 2002 by filing documents and providing his home address as its registered office, and he became a director and shareholder on 25 April 2003.
The defendant maintained that his $1 share in Webmoney was held as a nominee for his friend Luo Baocheng and that he did not invest money in or participate in Webmoney’s business.

The defendant communicated with former ABB technical expert Guenther Leonhardt between December 2002 and February 2003 regarding XIHARI’s interest in engaging him as a technical adviser and technical controller for a new generation of medium voltage products.

The defendant believed that XIHARI, as a Chinese government-owned research institute, could not pose a potential threat or competitive risk to the second and third plaintiffs, whose markets were Singapore and Southeast Asia.
The defendant believed that the ABB Group had no further interest in Mr Leonhardt’s services after his retirement and that assisting XIHARI to contact him would not harm the plaintiffs.
The defendant accepted an offer from Mr Liu in early 2003 to join Huadian as General Manager and to acquire up to 20% of Huadian’s shares at par value.
After joining Huadian, the defendant invited Mr Leonhardt to visit Huadian’s premises in Xiamen in March 2003, leading to a technical consultancy agreement between Huadian and Mr Leonhardt.
The defendant arranged for the incorporation of Gelpag mbH in Germany in October 2003, in which he held 85% of the shares and Mr Leonhardt held 10%, to act as a shareholder in Huadian.
Gelpag mbH purchased 16.2% of Huadian’s shares at par value in late March 2004, and the defendant’s investment in Gelpag mbH was funded in part by profits from the Xiamen property project.
The defendant maintained that he was not involved in the design or development of Huadian’s products and that his role was in general management and marketing.

The court accepted that the defendant was not a technical man, had not been involved in manufacturing ABB products, and had no access to confidential technical information on medium or high voltage products during his employment.

The court accepted that the main development of Huadian’s products was carried out by Yong Ying and XIHARI and that there was no evidence that the defendant supplied confidential ABB information to them.

The court found insufficient evidence that the defendant actively participated in Great Vision or the Xiamen project during his employment with the third plaintiff, beyond passing the opportunity to his brother.
The court found that Webmoney remained dormant and that the defendant’s limited involvement did not cause him to fail to give full and undivided attention to the plaintiffs’ business.

The court found that the defendant likely decided to join Huadian before leaving the third plaintiff and that he probably spent time before March 2003 preparing business plans and forecasts for Huadian.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendant’s conduct in relation to XIHARI, Huadian, Great Vision, Xiamen Fu Cheng, Webmoney, Gelpag International and Gelpag mbH breached the contractual, implied and fiduciary duties he owed to the plaintiffs during his employment, and whether he owed fiduciary duties to each of the three plaintiffs.
Party_Details.Rule: An employee’s contractual duties are determined by the express terms of his employment contracts and any incorporated policies, while fiduciary duties may arise for directors and very senior employees who occupy positions of trust and control; such fiduciary duties include duties of loyalty, good faith, avoidance of conflicts of interest, and disclosure of information and activities that may damage the company’s interests; an employee may plan to join a competing business but must not, while still employed, actively assist the competing business or take preparatory steps that conflict with his duty of fidelity; a holding company with no direct employment relationship may not be owed fiduciary duties by a senior employee of its subsidiaries.
Party_Details.Application: The court accepted the defendant’s position that the onerous obligation to promote the interests and welfare of the ABB Group did not carry over into the second and third contracts and that he did not owe fiduciary duties to the first plaintiff, a non-operating holding company; it found that the Employee Handbook was incorporated into his employment with the second and third plaintiffs and that his senior management roles gave rise to fiduciary duties to those entities; the court rejected the plaintiffs’ broader allegations that he misused confidential technical information or actively participated in the Xiamen property project and Webmoney in a way that harmed the plaintiffs, but it found that he breached his fiduciary duties and duty of fidelity to the second and third plaintiffs by failing to inform them of XIHARI’s intention to develop and market a new generation of medium voltage circuit breakers, by communicating with and assisting Mr Leonhardt to become a consultant for XIHARI and Huadian, and by spending time from about November 2002 working on plans to develop and run Huadian’s business while still employed by the third plaintiff.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The court held that the defendant did not owe fiduciary duties to the first plaintiff and that many of the plaintiffs’ broader allegations of breach were not established, but it concluded that the defendant was in breach of his fiduciary duties to the second and third plaintiffs and his duty of fidelity to the third plaintiff in relation to XIHARI and Huadian; judgment was entered against the defendant in favour of the second and third plaintiffs for either damages to be assessed or an account of profits to be taken, while the first plaintiff’s claim was dismissed.
